name: Greedy
tagline: Locally-optimal choices that are provably globally optimal.
concept:
## The idea

You already know dynamic programming solves optimization by checking **all possible** ways to break a problem into subproblems, storing results to avoid recomputation.

**Greedy** is simpler and faster: make the **locally best choice** at each step, never reconsider, and trust that this leads to a globally optimal answer. The catch: you must **prove** the greedy choice is safe — usually by showing that swapping any later choice with the greedy one can't improve the outcome (the "exchange argument"), or by showing the problem has "optimal substructure" where a local optimum chains into a global one.

When greedy works, it's beautiful: O(n) or O(n log n) (after sorting), no DP table, no recursion. When it doesn't, you need DP or backtracking. Recognizing the difference comes from practice — the problems below teach the signature cues.

## Python you'll pick up here

- Sorting: `arr.sort()`, `sorted(arr, key=lambda x: ...)` with custom keys
- `max(0, x)` to clamp negative values
- Dictionary with default: `d.get(k, 0)`, `collections.Counter`, `collections.defaultdict`
- List comprehensions: `[x for x in arr if condition]`
- `min()`/`max()` with generators
- Tuple unpacking in loops: `for start, end in intervals:`
whenToUse: The problem asks for a maximum/minimum and you can sort or scan left-to-right
Making the locally optimal choice at each step provably can't hurt the final answer
You can prove an exchange argument: swapping the greedy choice with any other never improves the result
The problem has optimal substructure: a local optimum plus the rest gives a global optimum

problems.title: Maximum Subarray
problems.keyIdeas: Kadane's algorithm is the canonical greedy intro: keep a running sum; if it drops below zero, discard it and start fresh. Why greedy? A negative prefix can never help a future sum — ditching it is always safe. This is also your first 1-D DP, but the greedy view is cleaner.
hints: Brute force checks every subarray — O(n²). Can you do better by scanning once?
Keep a running sum. What should you do when that sum becomes negative?
If current sum < 0, reset to 0. At each index, either extend the previous sum or start fresh from nums[i]. Track the max seen so far.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Check every possible subarray explicitly.

solutions.label: Optimal — Kadane's algorithm
solutions.keyIdea: Discard any prefix that goes negative; a negative prefix can never help a future sum.

solutions.label: Alternative — divide and conquer
solutions.keyIdea: Recursively split the array in half; the max subarray either lies entirely in the left half, entirely in the right half, or crosses the midpoint. For the crossing case, find the best suffix of the left and best prefix of the right. Reach for this when interviewer explicitly asks for a divide-and-conquer solution or O(n log n) approach; Kadane's is simpler and faster in practice.

problems.description: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.

problems.title: Jump Game
problems.keyIdeas: Track the farthest index you can reach as you scan left-to-right. At each index, update the farthest reachable. If you ever can't reach position `i` (i > farthest so far), return False. Reaching the last index means True. Greedy because you never need to backtrack — the farthest-reachable so far is all that matters.
hints: You don't need to track the exact jumps — just whether the end is reachable.
Track the farthest index you've proven reachable so far. For each index you can reach, update that max.
If you ever get to an index beyond the current max reachable, you're stuck. Otherwise, keep extending the frontier.

solutions.label: Optimal — greedy track max reach
solutions.keyIdea: Scan left to right, updating the farthest index we can reach. If we ever can't reach position i, we're stuck. Greedy is safe here because we only need reachability, not the path — the max reach so far encodes all possible futures.

solutions.label: Alternative — DP (top-down)
solutions.keyIdea: For each index, recursively check if any jump from it can reach the end, memoizing results. This is O(n²) in worst case because each index can try up to n jumps. Use greedy instead unless the problem asks for the actual jump sequence or has additional constraints that break the greedy property.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n^2) — each index may try up to n jumps

problems.description: You are given an integer array nums. You are initially positioned at the array's first index, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position.

problems.title: Jump Game II
problems.keyIdeas: BFS-style greedy: think of each jump as a level. Track the farthest you can reach in the current level; when you exhaust it, increment jump count and set the next level's boundary. You're always taking the fewest jumps because you sweep left-to-right in one pass, extending reach greedily.
hints: You want the *minimum* number of jumps. Can you think in terms of levels, like BFS?
Track two boundaries: the end of the current jump's range, and the farthest you can reach within it.
When you hit the end of the current range, increment jumps and extend the range to the farthest discovered.

solutions.label: Optimal — BFS-style greedy
solutions.keyIdea: Treat each jump as a BFS level; extend reach greedily within each level, then move to the next. Greedy works because taking the jump that reaches farthest never hurts — you can prove any earlier jump could be replaced by the farthest-reaching one without increasing the total jump count.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def jump(self, nums: list[int]) -> int:
        jumps = 0
        current_end = 0
        farthest = 0
        for i in range(len(nums) - 1):  # don't need to jump from the last index
            farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
            if i == current_end:
                jumps += 1
                current_end = farthest
        return jumps

solutions.label: Alternative — DP (bottom-up)
solutions.keyIdea: Build a table where dp[i] = minimum jumps to reach position i. For each position, update all reachable positions. This is O(n²) because each position may update up to n others. Reach for greedy instead; use DP when the problem has dependencies that break greedy (e.g., weighted jumps, obstacles that change over time).
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n^2) — each index may update up to n positions

problems.description: You are given a 0-indexed array of integers nums of length n. You are initially positioned at index 0.

problems.title: Gas Station
problems.keyIdeas: If total gas < total cost, no solution exists. Otherwise, scan once: track cumulative balance. When it goes negative, the problem is you started too early — so reset and try starting from the next station. The first start index that keeps balance ≥ 0 all the way to the end is the answer. Greedy because you can prove: if you can't complete from index i, you also can't complete from any j in [i+1, failing_point] — so skip straight past.
hints: First check if sum(gas) < sum(cost) — if so, no solution.
Scan once, tracking tank balance. When does the tank go negative?
If tank drops below zero at station j starting from i, you can prove you also can't complete starting from any station between i and j — so reset start to j+1.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Try starting from each station and simulate the full loop. First station that completes the circuit wins.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n^2) — try n starting points, each simulates n steps

solutions.label: Optimal — single-pass greedy
solutions.keyIdea: If total gas < total cost, no solution. Otherwise, scan once: if tank goes negative at station j starting from i, you can prove you can't start from any station in [i, j] either — so jump start to j+1. The greedy insight (exchange argument): if you fail at j starting from i, starting anywhere between i and j will fail even earlier because those positions have even less 'banked' gas. First candidate that never goes negative is the answer.

problems.description: There are n gas stations along a circular route, where the amount of gas at the ith station is gas[i].

problems.title: Hand of Straights
problems.keyIdeas: Greedy on sorted order: always try to form a group starting with the smallest remaining card. If you can't find the next `groupSize-1` consecutive cards, return False. Count each card, decrement as you use them. Why greedy? The smallest card *must* start some group — you can't defer it.
hints: If len(hand) isn't divisible by groupSize, return False immediately.
Sort the hand and count each card. Then try to form groups starting from the smallest card.
For each card, if its count > 0, try to decrement the counts of the next groupSize consecutive cards. If any is missing, return False.

solutions.label: Optimal — greedy with counter
solutions.keyIdea: The smallest card must start a group — it can't be deferred. Sort and count, then greedily form groups starting from the min card, decrementing counts. Exchange argument: if the smallest card doesn't start a group, it must appear later in some group, but then an even smaller card would have to come before it (contradiction). So greedy is safe.

solutions.label: Alternative — greedy with min-heap
solutions.keyIdea: Use a min-heap to always process the smallest card. Track ongoing groups in a second heap/queue. For each card, either extend an existing group (if it's consecutive) or start a new group. When a group is complete, remove it. Reach for this when cards arrive as a stream; the Counter approach is simpler for batch input.

problems.description: Alice has some number of cards and she wants to rearrange the cards into groups so that each group is of size groupSize, and consists of groupSize consecutive cards.

problems.title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet

video.title: NeetCode — Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: Greedy filter: any triplet with *any* element exceeding the target's corresponding element is useless (max-merge can't shrink it). Keep only triplets that don't exceed the target in any coordinate. Then check if the remaining triplets cover all three target values. One pass, O(n).
hints: Merging takes the max in each coordinate. Can a triplet with any value > target ever help?
Filter out triplets that exceed the target in any position. They're poison.
Among the remaining 'good' triplets, track whether you've seen each target coordinate at least once.

solutions.label: Optimal — greedy filter and coverage
solutions.keyIdea: Greedy insight: any triplet exceeding the target in any coordinate is poison (max-merge can never shrink it back). Filter those out, then check if the remaining 'good' triplets collectively cover all three target values. Greedy is safe because merge order doesn't matter — max is associative and commutative.

problems.description: A triplet is an array of three integers. You are given a 2D integer array triplets, where triplets[i] = [ai, bi, ci] describes the ith triplet. You are also given an integer array target = [x, y, z] that describes the triplet you want to obtain.

problems.title: Partition Labels
problems.keyIdeas: Precompute each character's last occurrence. Scan left-to-right, tracking the farthest last-occurrence in the current partition. When you reach that boundary, cut. Greedy because cutting as soon as possible maximizes the number of partitions — delaying the cut can't help.
hints: A partition is valid when every character in it doesn't appear outside. How can you tell where a character's range ends?
Build a map of each character's last index. As you scan, track the farthest last-index in the current partition.
When your scan index reaches the current partition's farthest-last, you can cut — no character in this partition appears beyond.

solutions.label: Optimal — greedy partition
solutions.keyIdea: Track the farthest last occurrence in the current partition; cut when you reach it.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def partitionLabels(self, s: str) -> list[int]:
        last = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(s)}  # last occurrence of each char
        result = []
        start = 0
        end = 0
        for i, c in enumerate(s):
            end = max(end, last[c])  # extend partition boundary
            if i == end:  # reached the boundary
                result.append(end - start + 1)
                start = i + 1
        return result

problems.description: You are given a string s. We want to partition the string into as many parts as possible so that each letter appears in at most one part. For example, the string "ababcc" can be partitioned into ["abab", "cc"], but partitions such as ["aba", "bcc"] or ["ab", "ab", "cc"] are invalid.

problems.title: Valid Parenthesis String
problems.keyIdeas: Track a *range* of possible open-paren counts as you scan. `(` increments both bounds, `)` decrements both, `*` can be anything so it widens the range. If the max possible count goes negative, impossible. At the end, check if 0 is in the valid range. Greedy because you track bounds instead of exploring all 3^(number of stars) assignments.
hints: Each `*` can be `(`, `)`, or empty — that's 3^k possibilities if there are k stars. Can you avoid enumerating all of them?
Track the *range* of possible open-paren counts: a min and max. How does each character affect these bounds?
`(` adds 1 to both, `)` subtracts 1 from both, `*` can add/subtract/do nothing — so it expands the range. Clamp min to 0 (can't go negative in valid strings). At the end, check if 0 is reachable.

solutions.label: Optimal — greedy range tracking
solutions.keyIdea: Maintain a range [min_open, max_open] of valid open-paren counts. `(` increments both, `)` decrements both, `*` widens the range (can be `(`, `)`, or empty). Greedy because we avoid enumerating 3^k assignments of stars — we just track bounds. Valid if 0 is reachable at the end. Reach for this over DP when you want O(1) space and the greedy insight is clear.

solutions.label: Alternative — DP (top-down)
solutions.keyIdea: Recursively try all three choices for each `*` (treat as `(`, `)`, or empty), memoizing (index, open_count) states. This explores 3^k star assignments but prunes with memoization. Use when the greedy range insight isn't obvious or when you need to track additional state (e.g., which stars were used as what).
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n^2) — n positions × n possible open counts

solutions.label: Alternative — two-pass stack
solutions.keyIdea: First pass left-to-right: treat `*` as `(` and ensure we can match all `)`. Second pass right-to-left: treat `*` as `)` and ensure we can match all `(`. Both must succeed. This validates that stars can be assigned to balance both directions. Use when you want an explicit validation in both directions; greedy range is more elegant.

problems.description: Given a string s containing only three types of characters: '(', ')' and '*', return true if s is valid.

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas
solutions.code:
def maximumMeetings(n,start,end):
    combined = [(start[i], end[i]) for i in range(n)]
    combined.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
    endTime = 0
    numMeetings = 0
    for slot in combined:
        if endTime<slot[0]:
            numMeetings += 1
            endTime = slot[1]
    return numMeetings
    
start = [10, 12, 20]
end =  [20, 25, 30]
print(maximumMeetings(len(start), start, end))

problems.title: max Profit In Unit Time Jobs
problems.keyIdeas: User function Template for python3     #Function to find the maximum profit and the number of jobs done.             #add future deadline jobs which are in heap if you can in extra slots if you can             #add all elements with currentDeadline to heap             #add max profit job in this slot
hints: Consider the constraints
Think about the optimal approach

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: User function Template for python3     #Function to find the maximum profit and the number of jobs done.             #add future deadline jobs which are in heap if you can in extra slots if you can             #add all elements with currentDeadline to heap             #add max profit job in this slot
solutions.code:
#User function Template for python3
import heapq
class Solution:
    
    #Function to find the maximum profit and the number of jobs done.
    def JobScheduling(self,jobs,n):
        jobs.sort(key=lambda x:x.deadline)
        numJobs, maxProfit, heap, index, nextDeadline = 0, 0, [], n-1, 0
        printJobs(jobs)
        while index>=0:
            currDeadline = jobs[index].deadline

            #add future deadline jobs which are in heap if you can in extra slots if you can
            numExtraSlots = nextDeadline - currDeadline - 1
            while heap and numExtraSlots>=1:
                maxProfitJobWithFutureDeadline = heapq.heappop(heap)
                maxProfit -= maxProfitJobWithFutureDeadline
                numJobs += 1
                numExtraSlots -= 1

            #add all elements with currentDeadline to heap
            while index>=0 and jobs[index].deadline==currDeadline:
                currDeadlineProfit = jobs[index].profit
                heapq.heappush(heap, -currDeadlineProfit)
                index -= 1
            
            #add max profit job in this slot   
            maxProfitJobWithAnyDeadline = heapq.heappop(heap)
            maxProfit -= maxProfitJobWithAnyDeadline
            numJobs += 1
            
            nextDeadline = currDeadline

        #add jobs till deadline = 0
        currDeadline = 0
        numExtraSlots = nextDeadline - currDeadline - 1
        while heap and numExtraSlots>=1:
            maxProfitJobWithFutureDeadline = heapq.heappop(heap)
            maxProfit -= maxProfitJobWithFutureDeadline
            numJobs += 1
            numExtraSlots -= 1
        return (numJobs, maxProfit)
